text: This is a fifty-five year old female with a past surgical history of a lap band placement, lap band removal due to intolerance, and a lap coli who was discovered to have a five centimeter sessile adenominous polyp in the cardi of the stomach, which was removed piecemeal endoscopically but recurred shortly after. After a discussion of the risks and benefits, she was taken to the OR for a robotic total gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y reconstruction using a twenty-five millimeter EEA stapler in a candy cane configuration.

Two months after surgery, the patient developed progressively worsening dysphagia. An EGD was performed demonstrating a stricture at the anastomosis, which required three CRE balloon dilations. An axial stent was then placed due to persistent dysphagia, which migrated and required a double balloon enteroscopy for retrieval, leading to a contained perforation.

Upon entry into the abdomen, the surgeon encounters multiple dense adhesions, including the left lobe of the liver adhered to the underlying bowel. Using careful dissection, the adhesions are separated to reach the esophagogegenostomy. A Nathanson liver retractor is used to free the roux limb from the left lateral segment of the liver, allowing access to the mediastinum. The dissection proceeds medially to laterally using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques.

The surgeon creates a posterior window and passes an umbilical tape for caudad retraction of the esophagus. A plane is identified anterior to the dilated esophagus, which is dissected down to reveal the right crus. Dense posterior adhesions are divided.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection of the mediastinal esophagus, which appears thickened and dilated, leading to the strictured anastomosis. The roux limb is identified, and the mesentery is divided approximately five centimeters downstream from the esophageal jejunostomy. The roux limb is then divided using a GIA-Sixty blue load stapler. Retraction stitches are placed at three and nine o'clock on the dilated esophagus, which is transected using shears. The tension-free jejunum is brought up, and an end-to-side esophago-jejunostomy is performed on the anti-mesenteric side.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis in a single layer using 2-0 Stratafix sutures, starting posteriorly and finishing anteriorly. An intraoperative endoscopy confirms a widely patent anastomosis with no air leaks.
